metadata.seed: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

patient.query: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

entailment: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and unsure of what’s next, and that can be really overwhelming.  
What are some moments, even small ones, when you’ve felt a sense of purpose or accomplishment?  
Exploring those experiences might help us see other ways you could build a sense of direction.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there have been a few moments. Like when I finished that big project at work last year, I was really proud of how it turned out. It felt good to see my ideas come together and the team appreciated my input. But then, it faded quickly, and I was left feeling like it was just... temporary.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you felt a strong sense of pride when the project finished, but then that feeling seemed to fade. What do you think made that pride feel temporary? Have you noticed any moments where the sense of accomplishment stayed with you longer, and if so, what was different about those times?

patient.query: Yeah, I think it was just this nagging feeling that I need to keep proving myself. I mean, I can get one thing done, but then there's always another deadline looming, you know? It’s like I just jump from one task to the next without really pausing to appreciate what I’ve done. I wish I could hold onto that pride a little longer, but it feels like there’s never enough time.

llm_response.response: I hear you feeling like you’re always chasing the next deadline and missing the chance to celebrate what you’ve already accomplished. What evidence do you have that you must keep proving yourself, and can you think of moments when you felt proud without needing to prove anything? How might you shift your focus to pause and acknowledge those achievements, even if just for a moment?

patient.query:
Yeah, it’s just... I guess I grew up thinking that I had to earn my place, you know? It feels like if I'm not constantly working, I’m falling behind. But there were those times in college when I just enjoyed the process of learning, like when I got into a project just for the sake of enjoying it and not worrying about the grades. I didn't feel that pressure then. 

Pausing and acknowledging things feels so hard. I mean, it’s like I’m scared

entailment: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure to keep moving forward. What would happen if you allowed yourself a brief pause to simply notice how you’re feeling? How do you know that not working right now means you’re falling behind?

patient.query: I don’t know... it just feels like if I stop, everything will pile up. Like if I take a break, people might think I’m not committed or that I'm slacking off. I guess I worry that maybe I’ll lose my edge. But at the same time, I can feel the burnout creeping in, and I don’t like that feeling either. It just makes me feel stuck.

neutral: Loss of interest in previously enjoyable activity (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that taking a break might make others think you’re not committed and that you’ll lose your edge, while at the same time you’re feeling the strain of burnout. What evidence do you have that people will judge you negatively if you pause, and how might a brief rest actually help you maintain or even improve your performance? Consider that rest can be a strategic move to preserve your motivation and prevent the burnout you’re already noticing.

patient.query: I guess I don’t really know if anyone would actually judge me... it’s more like this voice in my head that just says I should be doing something all the time. It’s hard to quiet that voice. And maybe a break would help me recharge, but I still worry that I’d fall behind or miss something important. It’s just so much easier to keep pushing, even when I know it’s exhausting. I feel like I'm caught in a loop.

llm_response.response: What evidence do you have that others would judge you if you took a break? How might a short pause change the way you feel about your responsibilities? What could happen if you allowed yourself a brief rest without fearing that you’ll fall behind?

patient.query:
I guess I don’t have any solid evidence... it’s just this feeling, you know? Sometimes I look around and see everyone working hard, and I feel like if I’m not in that same rhythm, I’ll stand out in a bad way. But maybe if I took a short break, I’d come back clearer or more focused? I don’t know, it’s hard to imagine. 

It just feels risky to step away, even for a moment. I worry I’d miss out

neutral: Loss of interest in previously enjoyable activity (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure to match everyone else’s pace, and that worry about standing out is weighing on you. What evidence do you have that stepping back would lead to missing out, and how might you test that idea in a small way? If you did take a brief pause, what might you notice that could help you decide whether it’s worth it?

patient.query:
Yeah, that pressure definitely feels like a heavy weight sometimes. I mean, the thought of slowing down just makes my stomach twist. I guess I have this idea that everyone else is somehow managing it all and if I take a step back, it’ll be like I’m not keeping up. 

But then there’s this little part of me that wonders if, maybe, just for a day or two, it wouldn’t be so disastrous. I don’t know... I could try taking a short break

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure feels like a heavy weight, and the thought of slowing down makes your stomach twist because you worry about falling behind. At the same time, a small part of you wonders if a brief pause might be possible. That tension between wanting to keep up and the curiosity about a break is understandable.

patient.query: Yeah, that’s pretty spot on. It’s like I’m pulled in two directions, and neither feels comfortable. I just keep pushing myself, but I’m also tired. It’s exhausting trying to keep up that image of always being on top of things. I don’t even know if it’s really worth it sometimes. Yet, the thought of letting go—even just a little—feels scary. What if it really does lead to falling behind? It’s confusing.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught between pushing hard and feeling exhausted, and you’re unsure if easing up might let you slip behind. What evidence do you have that letting go will actually cause you to fall behind? Have there been moments when you relaxed a bit and still managed to keep up? How could you test that idea on a small, low‑stakes level to see what actually happens?

patient.query:
Yeah, I guess I don’t really have hard evidence... it’s just a feeling that rules my thinking. I mean, there have been times when I’ve taken a weekend off and nothing bad happened at work. I just sort of went back to where I left off. But then there’s that little voice in my head that says, “What if this time is different?” 

Testing it on a small level sounds possible, but I immediately feel this anxiety about it. Like, what if I

llm_response.response: It sounds like that inner voice is raising a lot of “what if” scenarios that feel unsettling. What specific worries come up when you imagine a different outcome? How have you managed similar uncertainties in the past, and what did you learn from those experiences?
